text:
Lawyer Ezra is lead trial counsel for the plaintiff in a personal injury action. During pre-trial preparation, he realizes he personally witnessed the accident that forms the basis of the claim — he happened to be driving by and saw the collision. He is the only independent witness to the specific sequence of events. His client wants him to testify as a witness and remain as trial counsel. Ezra believes he can manage both roles professionally.

What must Ezra do under Rule 3.4-7 of the Rules of Professional Conduct?
opts: Ezra may act as both trial counsel and a witness if his client provides written informed consent to the dual role.

Ezra must withdraw as trial counsel if he is to testify as a witness at trial; Rule 3.4-7 provides that a lawyer must not act as advocate and witness in the same proceeding, except in limited circumstances such as testimony on uncontested matters; testifying on the key facts of the accident makes Ezra a material witness and triggers the withdrawal requirement.
Ezra may remain as trial counsel and also testify as a witness, provided he testifies at the beginning of the trial before beginning his role as advocate.
Ezra may remain as counsel and testify only if he offers to step aside from cross-examination of opposing witnesses to avoid the appearance of partiality.
expl: Rule 3.4-7 of the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct provides that a lawyer must not act as advocate in a proceeding in which the lawyer is likely to be called as a witness, unless certain narrow exceptions apply — such as when the evidence is uncontested or when withdrawal would work substantial hardship on the client. Testifying on the contested sequence of events in a personal injury accident is material, contested testimony that squarely engages the prohibition. Client consent does not override Rule 3.4-7 because the rule protects the administration of justice, not merely the client's interests.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 3.4-7 (lawyer as witness)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4 | Conflicts of Interest

text:
Lawyer Harriet is acting for a vendor in the sale of a small business. The purchaser has no lawyer and tells Harriet he understands the deal and does not need one. Harriet is eager to close the transaction quickly and, without advising the purchaser to seek independent legal advice, presents him with the purchase agreement and asks him to sign immediately. The purchaser signs and later realizes the agreement contained terms significantly unfavourable to him.

Did Harriet comply with her obligations under Rule 7.2-9 when dealing with the unrepresented purchaser?
opts: Yes — the purchaser stated he understood the deal and did not need a lawyer; Harriet had no further obligation once the purchaser declined representation.

No — Rule 7.2-9 requires a lawyer dealing with an unrepresented party to advise that party to obtain independent legal advice before proceeding; the purchaser's self-assessed understanding of the deal does not relieve Harriet of this obligation.
Yes — the obligation to seek independent legal advice rests with the party, not the opposing lawyer; Harriet had no duty to recommend counsel to the other side.
Yes — Harriet complied because she presented the agreement in writing, which gives the purchaser all the information needed to make an informed decision.
expl: Rule 7.2-9 of the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct requires a lawyer who deals with an unrepresented person to urge that person to obtain independent legal advice before entering into a transaction or agreement. The purchaser's statement that he did not need a lawyer does not discharge Harriet's obligation — the duty is on the lawyer to make the recommendation, not to accept the unrepresented person's self-assessment as a substitute. Harriet should have clearly advised the purchaser to seek independent legal advice, confirmed this was understood, and allowed time for the purchaser to do so before proceeding to execution.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 7.2-9 (duty to advise unrepresented party to seek independent legal advice)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8 | Dealing with Opposing Parties and Unrepresented Persons

text:
Lawyer Petra joins a new law firm, BrightLaw LLP. At her previous firm, Petra acted for DeltaCorp in a commercial dispute against Omega Ltd. BrightLaw currently represents Omega Ltd. in the same dispute. BrightLaw's managing partner says the firm can resolve the conflict by implementing an ethical screen around Petra — she will have no involvement in the Omega file, will be physically separated from the Omega team, will receive no financial benefit from the Omega matter, and will be instructed not to discuss the matter with anyone at the firm. BrightLaw proposes to put the screen in place immediately upon Petra's arrival.

Which of the following best describes the conditions under which an ethical screen may be effective for a migrating lawyer in Ontario under the Rules of Professional Conduct?
opts: Ethical screens are never effective in Ontario — a law firm cannot cure a migrating lawyer's conflict through any internal arrangement.

An ethical screen may be effective if it is erected immediately upon the lawyer's arrival at the new firm, the conflicted lawyer is fully excluded from the matter and receives no financial benefit from it, no confidential information has been shared, and the screen genuinely prevents any transfer of information; the screen must be timely, rigorous, and genuine.
An ethical screen is always effective for migrating lawyers — any internal barrier completely resolves all conflicts brought from a prior firm.
An ethical screen is effective only if the former client (DeltaCorp) provides written consent to the screen arrangement.
expl: The LSO Rules of Professional Conduct recognize that ethical screens can, in some circumstances, resolve conflicts arising from a lawyer migrating from one firm to another. The conditions for an effective screen include: the screen must be erected immediately upon the lawyer's arrival (not after confidential information has already been shared); the conflicted lawyer must be completely excluded from the matter; the conflicted lawyer must receive no financial benefit from the conflicted retainer; no confidential information may have transferred before the screen was established; and the screen must be genuine and rigorously maintained. Screens are not effective in all circumstances — if confidential information was already transmitted, or if the screen is implemented late or imperfectly, it will not cure the conflict.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 3.4 Commentary — ethical screens and migrating lawyers
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4 | Conflicts of Interest

text:
In a commercial real estate transaction, the purchaser's lawyer sends the vendor's lawyer funds from the purchaser in trust, with a trust condition letter stating: 'These funds are sent to you in trust on the condition that they are used only to discharge the existing mortgage on the property and pay any realty tax arrears. They are not to be released for any other purpose until the title is clear.' The vendor's lawyer's client instructs her to release the funds to the vendor immediately before the mortgage is discharged.

What is the legal status of the trust condition and what obligation does it impose on the vendor's lawyer?
opts: The trust condition is a courtesy communication only; it has no binding legal force unless it is in the form of a signed undertaking.

A trust condition received by a lawyer creates a binding obligation on the receiving lawyer to deal with the funds only in accordance with the terms of the condition; the vendor's lawyer is bound by the trust condition and must not release the funds contrary to its terms regardless of client instructions.
The trust condition is binding on the vendor client but not on the vendor's lawyer; the vendor's lawyer may follow her client's instructions to release the funds.
The vendor's lawyer may release the funds to the vendor if she provides a written undertaking to the purchaser's lawyer that the mortgage will be discharged within five business days.
expl: When a lawyer receives funds subject to a trust condition, the receiving lawyer is personally bound to deal with the funds only in accordance with the terms of the condition. A trust condition is not merely a letter of instruction to the client — it is a binding professional obligation on the receiving lawyer. If the vendor's lawyer accepts the funds subject to the trust condition, she must comply with its terms regardless of what her client instructs. She may not release the funds to the vendor before the mortgage is discharged. If she is unwilling to be bound by the condition, she must return the funds uncashed.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 7.2-11 (trust conditions — binding on receiving lawyer)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 10 | Undertakings

text:
Lawyer Xavier is representing a plaintiff in a civil action. Frustrated by what he sees as excessive delays in scheduling a motion, he telephones the chambers of the presiding judge directly without notice to opposing counsel to request an urgent hearing date and to explain his concerns about the scheduling process. The judge's assistant takes a message. When opposing counsel later learns of the call, she objects.

Has Xavier engaged in improper ex parte communication with the court, and is the objection well-founded?
opts: No — contacting the court's scheduling office or chambers to request a hearing date is an administrative matter that is not subject to the prohibition on ex parte communications.

Yes — any direct communication with the court, including to scheduling staff or chambers, without notice to opposing counsel regarding the substance of the proceeding is improper ex parte communication; Xavier should have communicated through proper channels, such as a consent motion or scheduling conference notice to all parties.
No — ex parte communications with the court are only prohibited when they are made in writing; an oral telephone message does not violate any rule.
No — because Xavier did not actually speak with the judge, no ex parte communication occurred.
expl: The prohibition on ex parte communications with the court applies to any substantive communication with the court or judicial staff about a proceeding, without the knowledge of opposing counsel. Contacting chambers to complain about scheduling and request a hearing is not a purely administrative scheduling request — it involves conveying the lawyer's view of the scheduling process to judicial staff, which is a substantive communication about the matter. All parties are entitled to be present when any such communication is made. Xavier should have written to all parties and used the court's formal scheduling procedures to address his concerns.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 5.1-2 (ex parte communications with court)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7 | Duties to Tribunals and Third Parties

text:
Rashida is a corporate lawyer who personally holds a significant equity interest in TechStart Inc., a startup company. A client approaches Rashida and asks her to provide legal advice on whether the client should invest in TechStart Inc. in its next funding round. The client wants independent advice on the merits of the investment.

What type of conflict exists and how is it assessed under Rule 3.4-1?
opts: No conflict exists — Rashida and the client have aligned interests because they both want TechStart to succeed; aligned interests cannot constitute a conflict.

Rashida has an own-interest conflict under Rule 3.4-1; her personal financial stake in TechStart creates a substantial risk that her advice to the client about whether to invest may be materially affected by her own desire for the startup to raise funds.
No conflict — lawyers are routinely permitted to have personal investments in client businesses without disclosure or consent obligations.
A conflict exists only if Rashida actually profits from the client's investment decision — until profit is realized, the conflict is merely theoretical.
expl: Rule 3.4-1 identifies own-interest conflicts as a type of conflict where the lawyer's personal interests may materially and adversely affect the representation. Rashida's financial stake in TechStart creates a substantial risk that she will not provide fully objective advice about whether the client should invest — her personal interest in the startup's fundraising success will colour her analysis. This conflict must be disclosed and may require declining the retainer.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 3.4-1
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4 | Conflicts of Interest

text:
Lawyer Tanya has been acting for a plaintiff in a personal injury trial that is in its fifth day. The client owes $40,000 in unpaid fees. Tanya has given proper written notice to the client three weeks earlier that she would withdraw if fees were not paid, but no payment has been made. The client has been under cross-examination by opposing counsel for two days and is expected to be excused from the stand tomorrow. Tanya now wants to withdraw mid-trial.

May Tanya withdraw mid-trial at this point on the basis of non-payment, given that she gave proper advance notice?
opts: Yes — Tanya gave proper notice three weeks in advance; she has fulfilled all requirements for withdrawal and may now withdraw at any point, including mid-trial.
Yes — non-payment of fees is an absolute ground for withdrawal that overrides any timing concerns once proper notice has been given.

No — even though non-payment is a valid ground for withdrawal and proper notice was given, Rule 3.7-1 requires that a lawyer not withdraw at a time or in a manner that would cause prejudice to the client; withdrawing mid-trial while the client is under cross-examination is precisely the kind of prejudicial timing the rule prohibits unless mandatory grounds exist.
No — a lawyer can never withdraw from a civil trial once it has started, regardless of the grounds.
expl: Rule 3.7-1 of the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct recognizes non-payment as a ground for optional withdrawal but requires that the withdrawal be timed so as not to cause prejudice to the client. Withdrawing while the client is mid-cross-examination in a personal injury trial is extraordinarily prejudicial — it would leave the client without counsel at the most critical and vulnerable moment of the proceeding. The fact that Tanya gave prior notice does not override the timing constraint. Proper notice three weeks ago may have been sufficient for a transactional matter, but litigation requires that timing minimize prejudice. Mandatory withdrawal grounds (such as client fraud) could justify withdrawal even mid-trial, but non-payment is only a permissive ground and carries the additional constraint of appropriate timing.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 3.7-1 (withdrawal — timing constraint — no prejudice to client)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 9 | Withdrawal from Representation

text:
Lawyer Mira runs a personal injury law firm. Her firm's website states: 'Mira's Law — We Win 98% of Our Cases. When You Win, We Win. Call Us for Your Guaranteed Settlement.' The 98% statistic is based on a sample of 50 small claims files her firm resolved in the past year; the firm's overall success rate including all cases across all years is not reflected in the statistic. The 'guaranteed settlement' language is intended to mean the firm works on contingency.

Which of the following best describes the professional responsibility issues with Mira's advertisement?
opts: The advertisement is compliant because the 98% statistic, while based on a sample, is technically accurate for those files.
The advertisement is compliant because 'guaranteed settlement' is a recognized marketing expression that clients understand does not mean a literal guarantee.

The advertisement breaches Rule 4.2-1 in multiple respects: the 98% statistic is misleading because it is based on an unrepresentative sample and implies a general success rate that does not exist; and the 'guaranteed settlement' language is prohibited because lawyers cannot guarantee outcomes or results.
The advertisement breaches Rule 4.2-1 only if a client actually relies on the misleading information and suffers harm.
expl: Rule 4.2-1 and its Commentary prohibit lawyers from making advertising claims that are misleading, even if technically true in a narrow sense. Using a statistic based on an unrepresentative sample of 50 small claims files to imply a 98% general success rate is misleading — it does not accurately represent the firm's overall performance. More critically, the Commentary to Rule 4.2-1 expressly prohibits guaranteeing outcomes or results. Language such as 'guaranteed settlement' is prohibited regardless of what the lawyer intends it to mean, because it creates a misleading impression that a favourable result is assured. This is an exam trap: candidates may focus on the technical truth of the statistic and miss that misleading-by-omission and result guarantees are prohibited.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 4.2-1 Commentary (misleading advertising; guaranteeing results)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 13 | Marketing, Advertising, and Discrimination

text:
Lawyer Theo works in private practice as a sole practitioner. His LAWPRO errors and omissions insurance policy lapses because he forgot to pay the annual premium. He does not realize the lapse for three weeks and continues to practise law during that period. A client files a complaint alleging that Theo provided advice that caused financial loss during the period the insurance was lapsed.

Which of the following best describes Theo's professional responsibility exposure arising from practising without insurance coverage?
opts: Theo has no professional responsibility exposure — E&O insurance is a private contractual matter between Theo and LAWPRO and is not governed by LSO By-Laws.

Theo is exposed to professional discipline for practising without the mandatory LAWPRO errors and omissions insurance required under By-Law 6; all licensees in private practice must maintain continuous E&O coverage, and practising without it during the lapse period is a breach of licensing obligations.
Theo has exposure only if he is actually found liable to the client — until liability is established, the insurance lapse creates no disciplinary risk.
Theo's coverage lapse is automatically reinstated by LAWPRO upon payment of the overdue premium, as if the coverage never lapsed.
expl: By-Law 6 of the LSO By-Laws requires all licensees engaged in private practice to maintain errors and omissions insurance through LAWPRO, the Law Society's mandatory insurance program. Maintaining continuous coverage is a licensing obligation, not merely a prudential business decision. Allowing the policy to lapse — even inadvertently — and continuing to practise during the lapse constitutes a breach of By-Law 6 and exposes Theo to professional discipline. The existence or non-existence of an actual client claim is irrelevant to the licensing obligation.
caseRef: LSO By-Law 6 (mandatory errors and omissions insurance — LAWPRO)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 1 | Licensing and Regulation of the Legal Profession

text:
Lawyer Colin opens a trust account at an online-only bank incorporated in the United States that offers high interest rates on deposits. He uses this account to hold client funds from his Ontario practice, reasoning that since the client funds are protected by the high interest rate, the location of the bank is irrelevant.

Does By-Law 9 permit Colin to maintain his trust account at a foreign online-only bank?
opts: Yes — as long as client funds are safe and earn a good interest rate, the location or type of financial institution is irrelevant under By-Law 9.

No — By-Law 9 requires that a lawyer's trust account be maintained at a recognized financial institution in Ontario; a foreign bank, even one with a strong interest rate, does not satisfy this requirement.
Yes — online banks are recognized financial institutions under By-Law 9 regardless of their country of incorporation.
No — trust accounts must be maintained at one of the five major Canadian chartered banks specifically listed in By-Law 9.
expl: By-Law 9 requires that a lawyer's trust account be held at a recognized financial institution in Ontario. The financial institution must be authorized to accept deposits and must operate in Ontario. A foreign bank incorporated in the United States — even if it offers competitive interest rates — does not qualify as a recognized Ontario financial institution under By-Law 9. Maintaining client trust funds at an unapproved foreign institution violates the trust account requirements and may expose client funds to regulatory and legal risks outside the Ontario framework.
caseRef: LSO By-Law 9 (trust account — recognized financial institution in Ontario)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6 | Trust Accounting and Financial Management

text:
Lawyer Adriana represents a client who is a demolition contractor. During a privileged meeting, the client reveals that he has been deliberately falsifying safety certifications for buildings he has already demolished and that the debris has been mixed with clean fill and sold to two residential development sites where construction is now underway. Adriana believes the contaminated fill may cause structural failure of residential buildings and serious physical harm to future occupants. The client refuses to stop or disclose.

Under Rule 3.3-3, may Adriana disclose this information to prevent serious bodily harm to future occupants of the affected buildings?
opts: No — the harm is speculative because the buildings have not yet been constructed and no one has been injured.

Yes — Rule 3.3-3 permits Adriana to disclose confidential information to the extent reasonably necessary to prevent what she believes on reasonable grounds to be a criminal act likely to result in serious bodily harm; the use of contaminated fill creating structural failure risk meets this threshold.
No — Rule 3.3-3 only applies to direct, immediate threats of violence by the client against an identified individual.
Yes, but only if Adriana first obtains a court order authorizing the disclosure.
expl: Rule 3.3-3 permits a lawyer to disclose confidential information where the lawyer believes on reasonable grounds that disclosure is necessary to prevent a criminal act likely to result in death or serious bodily harm. The rule does not require the harm to be immediate or to involve a named victim — it requires that the harm be likely and arise from a criminal act. Falsifying safety certifications and selling contaminated fill for use in residential construction is a criminal act that creates a reasonable risk of structural failure and serious bodily harm to future residents. The rule is permissive (Adriana may disclose) rather than mandatory, and disclosure must be limited to what is necessary to prevent the harm.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 3.3-3
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 3 | Confidentiality

text:
Lawyer Zelda is acting for a client who alleges that a business associate defrauded her of $50,000 in a commercial dispute. Zelda sends a letter to the business associate — who is not represented by counsel — stating: 'Unless you pay my client $50,000 within 10 days, my client will instruct me to report you to the police for fraud and we will pursue the maximum criminal penalties available.' The civil claim has not yet been filed.

Does Zelda's letter violate Rule 7.2-7 of the Rules of Professional Conduct?
opts: No — a lawyer is entitled to inform an opposing party of the legal options available to the client, including criminal reporting, as part of legitimate settlement demands.

Yes — Rule 7.2-7 prohibits a lawyer from threatening to initiate criminal proceedings against a person primarily as a means to obtain a civil advantage; using the threat of a criminal complaint to coerce payment of a civil debt crosses the line from legitimate settlement demand to improper threat.
No — because the business associate is not represented by counsel, Zelda is entitled to be more direct in explaining the consequences of non-payment.
No — Rule 7.2-7 only applies after a civil action has been filed; pre-litigation threats are not covered by the rule.
expl: Rule 7.2-7 of the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct prohibits a lawyer from threatening to initiate criminal or regulatory proceedings against a person primarily as a device to extract a civil concession or payment. The letter Zelda sent explicitly conditions the withholding of a criminal complaint on payment of the civil debt, which is precisely the conduct the rule prohibits. This is distinct from legitimately advising a client on whether to report criminal conduct to the police — the prohibited conduct is using the threat as a settlement lever. The rule applies regardless of the merits of any potential criminal complaint and regardless of whether a civil action has been filed.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 7.2-7 (threatening criminal proceedings to gain civil advantage)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8 | Dealing with Opposing Parties and Unrepresented Persons

text:
Beatrice is a senior litigation partner. She assigns a complex interlocutory motion to her articling student, providing no instructions, no checklist, and no supervision. The articling student files a factum that is legally deficient and fails to address the key issues. As a result, the client's motion is dismissed and the client suffers significant procedural prejudice.

Which statement best describes Beatrice's obligations under Rule 3.1-2?
opts: Only the articling student is responsible for the inadequate factum because the student was the one who drafted it.

Beatrice breaches Rule 3.1-2 by failing to supervise the articling student; a lawyer must ensure that work performed by subordinate staff meets the standard of competence required of the lawyer directly performing the work.
Beatrice has no obligation to supervise articling students who are separately overseen by the Law Society of Ontario through the licensing process.
Beatrice's competence obligation is satisfied because she was available to review the factum if the student had asked.
expl: The competence obligation under Rule 3.1-2 extends to work delegated to subordinate staff. A lawyer who delegates legal work remains responsible for ensuring it meets the standard of competence. Failure to provide supervision or instructions when delegating complex work is itself a breach of the competence rule.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 3.1-2
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 | Competence and Quality of Service

text:
A client believes her lawyer handled her estate matter incompetently and also charged excessive fees. She wants to take formal action. A colleague tells her she must sue the lawyer in court and that the Law Society only deals with criminal behaviour by lawyers. Another colleague tells her the Law Society will reimburse her directly if the complaint is successful.

Which of the following most accurately describes the LSO complaints process and what it can achieve for the client?
opts: The LSO complaints process is only available to clients who have suffered financial loss; complaints about service quality or excessive fees are not investigated.

The LSO complaints process allows any person to file a complaint about a lawyer's conduct; the LSO investigates complaints of professional misconduct, conduct unbecoming a licensee, or incompetence; a successful complaint may result in regulatory consequences for the lawyer, but the LSO complaints process does not award damages or directly reimburse the complainant for financial losses.
Only the Law Society itself may initiate complaints against lawyers; members of the public cannot file complaints directly.
The LSO complaints process automatically awards compensation to clients whose lawyers are found to have engaged in professional misconduct.
expl: The Law Society Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. L.8 establishes the LSO complaints and discipline process. Any person — including clients, opposing parties, judges, and members of the public — may file a complaint with the LSO about a lawyer's conduct. The LSO investigates complaints of professional misconduct, conduct unbecoming a licensee, or incompetence. A successful complaint may result in regulatory outcomes such as reprimand, conditions on practice, suspension, or revocation. However, the LSO complaints process is a regulatory proceeding — it does not award damages to complainants. If the client wants monetary compensation, she must pursue a civil claim or apply to the Lawyers' Fund for Client Compensation in eligible cases.
caseRef: Law Society Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. L.8, ss. 34-49 (complaints and discipline process)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 11 | Complaints and Discipline

text:
Lawyer Kwame briefly met with a potential client, Hartley, four years ago for a 30-minute initial consultation. No retainer was formed and no legal services were provided. Hartley disclosed that he was generally involved in the import business and was concerned about a potential commercial dispute with a supplier. No specific confidential information about the dispute was provided. Kwame now receives a request from a company to sue Hartley in an unrelated commercial matter involving a real estate lease. Kwame assumes he cannot act because he met with Hartley previously.

Is Kwame correct that his prior consultation with Hartley automatically bars him from acting against Hartley?
opts: Yes — any prior meeting with a person, even without a formal retainer, creates an automatic former-client conflict that bars subsequent adverse representation.

No — the test under Rule 3.4-10 is whether Kwame received confidential information from Hartley that is relevant to the new matter; a brief consultation in which only general business information was shared and no retainer formed may not create a conflict, particularly where the new matter is entirely unrelated; Kwame must carefully assess whether any relevant confidential information was received.
Yes — the duty of loyalty to former clients applies regardless of whether confidential information was actually received.
No — because no retainer was formed, Kwame has no professional obligations to Hartley whatsoever and the Rules of Professional Conduct do not apply.
expl: Rule 3.4-10 asks whether the lawyer received confidential information from the former client that is relevant to the new matter. The rule does not impose a blanket prohibition on any prior contact. A brief initial consultation in which only general business background was shared, no retainer was formed, and the new matter is entirely unrelated may not generate a former-client conflict. The exam trap is assuming that any prior meeting automatically disqualifies the lawyer. Kwame must analyze whether the specific information he received is relevant to the new real estate dispute — if not, the conflict may not exist. However, if any specific confidential information was received even in the consultation, the analysis changes.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 3.4-10 (former client — relevant confidential information test)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4 | Conflicts of Interest

text:
Lawyer Marissa is representing a plaintiff in a personal injury action. In her written submissions, she accurately describes the medical evidence supporting the plaintiff's injuries but deliberately omits a subsequent medical report — prepared at her client's own request — that concluded the plaintiff had made a full recovery and had no ongoing impairment. Marissa reasons that the omitted report was unfavourable, that opposing counsel has a copy, and that candour does not require her to highlight evidence against her own client.

Has Marissa breached her duty of candour under Rule 5.1-2 by omitting the recovery report from her submissions?
opts: No — a lawyer has no obligation to highlight evidence that undermines her client's case; the adversarial system places the burden on opposing counsel to adduce contrary evidence.
No — because opposing counsel has the report, the court is not being misled; candour is only violated when the court has no access to the omitted information.

Yes — Rule 5.1-2 prohibits a lawyer from creating a false impression in the mind of the tribunal through selective presentation of facts; deliberately omitting a material medical report that contradicts the plaintiff's injury claim misleads the tribunal by omission, even if opposing counsel possesses the report.
No — the duty of candour applies only to statements of law, not to the selection of factual evidence presented in submissions.
expl: Rule 5.1-2 of the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct prohibits a lawyer from creating a false impression in the mind of the tribunal through selective presentation, omission of material facts, or misleading innuendo. Candour extends beyond avoiding outright lies — it encompasses not structuring submissions so as to convey a misleading picture of the evidence. The fact that opposing counsel has the report does not remedy the misleading impression created by Marissa's selective submissions. The court must be presented with a fair, not strategically distorted, account of the material facts.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 5.1-2 (misleading by omission — selective presentation of facts)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7 | Duties to Tribunals and Third Parties

text:
Lawyer Dario posts on a legal blog about a 'hypothetical' scenario involving a technology startup facing regulatory scrutiny over its data-collection practices, the industry sector, the approximate revenue level, the jurisdiction, and a distinctive business model. He does not name the client. Dario is currently representing a client that precisely matches all these details. He believes his post is acceptable because he changed the client's name and called it a hypothetical.

Has Dario breached the duty of confidentiality by posting this 'anonymized hypothetical' about his client?
opts: No — removing the client's name is sufficient anonymization to satisfy the duty of confidentiality.
No — a lawyer may discuss legal scenarios in educational or professional contexts as long as the client is not identified by name.

Yes — the duty of confidentiality under Rule 3.3-1 is breached where a post contains sufficient identifying details that persons familiar with the client could recognize them; 'anonymization' that leaves industry, jurisdiction, revenue level, and distinctive business model intact is likely insufficient; confidentiality protects client information from disclosure, not merely from named disclosure.
Yes, but only if the client suffers actual harm from the disclosure.
expl: Rule 3.3-1 protects all information acquired in the professional relationship — not merely the client's name. Publishing information that, taken together, is sufficiently distinctive to identify the client constitutes a breach of confidentiality even if the name is omitted. This is sometimes called 'jigsaw identification' — combining multiple details can uniquely identify a client even without explicit naming. The test is not whether Dario named the client, but whether a reader familiar with the client could identify them. A lawyer wishing to use client scenarios for educational purposes must obtain client consent or ensure the scenario is genuinely unidentifiable.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 3.3-1; Commentary on social media and confidentiality
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 3 | Confidentiality

text:
In a civil dispute, Lawyer Faye acts for the plaintiff. In exchange for the plaintiff releasing original business records to the defendant's lawyer on a without-prejudice basis, opposing counsel gives Faye a written undertaking to return all original documents within 30 days regardless of the outcome of any negotiations. Thirty-five days later, the documents have not been returned and opposing counsel has stopped responding to Faye's requests. Faye asks her client how to enforce the undertaking.

What remedies are available to Faye when the opposing lawyer fails to honor a written undertaking to return documents?
opts: Faye's only remedy is to commence a civil action against the opposing lawyer for breach of contract; the LSO does not handle disputes about documents between lawyers.

Faye may file a complaint with the LSO reporting the breach of undertaking, which is professional misconduct; she may also bring a summary court application to enforce the undertaking directly without requiring full civil litigation; additionally, damages may be recoverable if the client has suffered loss from the breach.
Faye should write a strongly worded letter to the opposing lawyer; if that does not work, she must wait until the civil proceedings are complete before seeking enforcement of the undertaking.
Faye may only seek enforcement through the LSO complaints process; courts have no jurisdiction to enforce undertakings between lawyers because they are professional obligations, not legal contracts.
expl: When an opposing lawyer fails to honor a written undertaking, the aggrieved party has multiple avenues of redress. First, the breach of undertaking is professional misconduct, and a complaint may be filed with the LSO. Second, Ontario courts have recognized their inherent jurisdiction to summarily enforce lawyers' undertakings — Faye can bring a court application directly without going through full civil litigation. This is a well-established remedy that allows rapid enforcement. Third, if the client has suffered damages from the breach (such as loss caused by inability to use the original documents), civil damages may be recoverable. All three remedies may be pursued simultaneously.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 7.2-11 (breach of undertaking — LSO complaint, court enforcement, damages)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 10 | Undertakings

text:
Lawyer Yasmine is representing a landlord in an application before the Landlord and Tenant Board. She is aware of a binding provincial court decision that directly contradicts the statutory interpretation she is advancing, which was decided just three weeks earlier. Opposing counsel — a self-represented tenant — has not cited this decision and appears unaware of it. Yasmine decides not to mention it.

Does Yasmine have an obligation to disclose the contrary binding decision to the Landlord and Tenant Board even though opposing counsel has not raised it?
opts: No — in an adversarial system, a lawyer has no obligation to cite authorities that harm the client's case; discovering and citing helpful authorities is the opposing party's responsibility.

Yes — Rule 5.1-2 requires a lawyer as advocate to disclose to the tribunal any binding legal authority directly relevant to the matter that is adverse to the client's position, regardless of whether the opposing party has found it; the self-represented status of the tenant does not change this obligation.
No — the duty to disclose adverse legal authorities only applies in courts, not before administrative tribunals like the Landlord and Tenant Board.
No — the decision was issued only three weeks ago and is too recent to be considered binding; Yasmine has no obligation to cite newly released decisions.
expl: Rule 5.1-2 of the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct requires a lawyer as advocate to cite relevant binding legal authorities known to the lawyer, even if unfavourable to the client's position. This duty applies before all tribunals, including administrative bodies like the Landlord and Tenant Board. The fact that the opposing party is self-represented and has not found the authority does not relieve Yasmine of the obligation — the duty runs to the tribunal, not to the opposing party. A recently issued decision is binding if it meets the applicable binding authority standard.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 5.1-2 (disclosure of adverse binding authority)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7 | Duties to Tribunals and Third Parties

text:
Lawyer Yvonne acted for a client in a wrongful dismissal action. The action settled for $80,000, which was deposited to trust. Yvonne's account for fees and disbursements is $25,000. Before disbursing funds to the client, Yvonne deducts $25,000 from the trust account representing her fees and sends the remaining $55,000 to the client. The client had not provided a written direction but had verbally agreed to the deduction after seeing Yvonne's account.

Which concept allows Yvonne to assert priority over the settlement funds in trust for payment of her fees, and what additional step should she have taken?
opts: Yvonne has no right to deduct fees from settlement funds in trust under any circumstances; all proceeds must go to the client first.

Yvonne may assert a charging lien (equitable lien) over the proceeds of the action she helped obtain for the client; however, she should have obtained a written direction from the client before deducting fees from trust to fully comply with By-Law 9.
Yvonne is entitled to deduct fees from settlement funds as soon as she renders her account because delivering the account constitutes sufficient written authorization under By-Law 9.
Yvonne may retain the $25,000 from trust because the client verbally agreed, and verbal consent is sufficient authorization for trust withdrawals under By-Law 9.
expl: A solicitor's charging lien is an equitable right that attaches to money or property recovered for the client through the lawyer's efforts, including funds held in trust representing settlement proceeds. The charging lien entitles the lawyer to be paid from those proceeds before they are distributed to the client. However, the mechanics of withdrawing from trust are governed by By-Law 9, which requires a written direction from the client (or a court order) before fees may be deducted from trust. A verbal agreement and a rendered account, while consistent with the equitable charging lien concept, do not satisfy By-Law 9's written direction requirement.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO); LSO By-Law 9, s. 9 (trust withdrawal — written direction); common law solicitor's charging lien
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 5 | Fees, Billing, and Financial Dealings with Clients

text:
Lawyer Cedric runs a busy personal injury practice. He has an informal arrangement with his office manager, who is not a lawyer or paralegal, whereby the office manager receives 5% of the fee from any client the office manager personally recruited for the firm. Cedric views this as a performance bonus tied to business development results.

Is Cedric's fee-sharing arrangement with his office manager permissible under the Rules of Professional Conduct?
opts: Yes — sharing a percentage of fees with a non-lawyer employee is permissible as a performance incentive as long as it is called a 'bonus' rather than a 'fee split.'

No — sharing legal fees with a non-licensee, even under the label of a 'bonus,' is prohibited under the Rules of Professional Conduct; a fee share tied to specific legal fees earned from clients the employee recruits is a prohibited fee split with a non-lawyer.
Yes — an office manager is an employee of the firm and may receive a percentage of fees as compensation.
No — but only because the 5% share exceeds the maximum permitted non-lawyer bonus under By-Law 9.
expl: The Rules of Professional Conduct prohibit sharing legal fees with non-licensees. The prohibition extends beyond arrangements expressly called 'fee splits' — it applies whenever a non-lawyer receives compensation calculated as a share of specific legal fees earned from particular client files. Calling the payment a 'bonus' does not change its character if it is in fact a percentage of legal fees tied to specific files or clients the employee brought in. An office manager may receive a salary, benefits, and discretionary performance bonuses tied to general business success, but may not receive a percentage of the legal fees earned from specific client files.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 3.6-6 (fee splitting — prohibition with non-licensees)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 5 | Fees, Billing, and Financial Dealings with Clients

text:
Lawyer Tariq holds $12,000 in trust for a client whose commercial lease negotiation has concluded. Tariq has done the work and intends to charge $8,000 but has not yet prepared a formal invoice. He needs cash immediately and transfers $8,000 from the client's trust account to his general account, planning to send the invoice to the client later that week.

Has Tariq complied with By-Law 9's requirements for withdrawing fees from trust?
opts: Yes — the work is done and the entitlement to fees exists; Tariq may transfer the funds and send the invoice afterward.
No — By-Law 9 requires that a lawyer deliver an account (invoice) to the client before or at the same time as withdrawing fees from trust; withdrawing fees before billing is not permitted.
Yes — because Tariq intends to send the invoice that same week, the timing is close enough to satisfy By-Law 9.
No — but only because Tariq did not get a written direction from the client; if he had a written direction, taking fees before billing would be permissible.
expl: By-Law 9 requires that a lawyer deliver an account (invoice) to the client before withdrawing fees from trust. The entitlement to be paid — based on having completed the work — does not authorize a lawyer to take money from trust before billing. The account must be rendered first, so that the client has notice of and an opportunity to review the fees being charged before the funds leave trust. The written direction from the client (if required) also presupposes that the client has seen an account. Tariq's transfer of funds before issuing an invoice violates By-Law 9.
caseRef: LSO By-Law 9 (trust withdrawal — account must be delivered before withdrawal)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6 | Trust Accounting and Financial Management

text:
Lawyer Brett receives a package of confidential financial records from opposing counsel in a civil dispute, accompanied by a trust condition letter stating: 'These documents are produced to you on the condition that they will be used solely for the purposes of this litigation and will not be shared with any third parties or used for any collateral purpose.' Brett reviews the documents and realizes they contain highly useful information for a different matter he is handling for a related client. He decides to use the documents for that other matter while retaining them for the current litigation.

Is Brett's use of the documents for the collateral matter consistent with the trust condition imposed when the documents were delivered?
opts: Yes — once documents are received by a lawyer in litigation, they may be used for any legitimate legal purpose; trust conditions are advisory only and cannot restrict how a receiving lawyer uses documents.

No — a trust condition received by a lawyer is a binding professional obligation; Brett must use the documents only for the purpose specified in the condition; using them for any other matter is a breach of the trust condition and may constitute professional misconduct.
Yes — as long as Brett keeps the documents confidential and does not share them externally, he may use them for related matters; the trust condition only restricts disclosure to third parties.
No — but Brett's only obligation is to return the documents once they are no longer needed for the current litigation; he may use them for the collateral matter in the interim.
expl: A trust condition received by a lawyer creates a binding professional obligation on the receiving lawyer to use the materials only in accordance with the terms of the condition. The condition here is clear: sole use for this litigation, no use for collateral purposes. Brett's use of the documents for a different matter directly violates the trust condition. If Brett is unwilling to be bound by the conditions, his obligation is to return the documents immediately — he cannot accept the documents while rejecting the conditions attached to them. Using the documents in violation of the trust condition is a breach of a professional obligation and may expose Brett to professional misconduct proceedings.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 7.2-11 (trust conditions — receiving documents — must comply or return)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 10 | Undertakings

text:
Lawyer Gianna acted for both a lender and borrower in documenting a personal loan between family members. Before proceeding she provided full Rule 3.4-5 disclosures to both clients. She then recommended that both clients seek independent legal advice. The borrower, an elderly parent, was told by Gianna: 'You can get another lawyer to look at this if you want, but it's a simple document.' The parent did not seek other advice. The parent later claims the loan terms were unfair and that Gianna pressured them into signing.

Was Gianna's recommendation of independent legal advice adequate under Rule 3.4-5?
opts: Yes — Gianna recommended independent legal advice and it was the client's choice not to obtain it; the lawyer's obligation ends with the recommendation.

No — the recommendation of independent legal advice under Rule 3.4-5 must be genuine and unequivocal; qualifying the recommendation with 'if you want' and characterizing the agreement as 'simple' may have discouraged the client from actually obtaining advice, undermining the purpose of the requirement.
Yes — because both parties received the Rule 3.4-5 disclosures, the independent legal advice recommendation is merely aspirational.

No — Rule 3.4-5 requires that the client actually obtain independent legal advice before the joint retainer may proceed; the client's failure to obtain advice means the joint retainer was impermissible.
expl: The requirement to recommend independent legal advice under Rule 3.4-5 must be a genuine, substantive recommendation — not a perfunctory mention undermined by language that discourages uptake. Telling a client 'you can get another lawyer if you want' while simultaneously characterizing the document as 'simple' effectively signals that obtaining advice is unnecessary, defeating the protective purpose of the requirement. The purpose of the independent legal advice recommendation is to ensure the client understands the nature and risks of the arrangement from an independent perspective. Gianna's approach failed to achieve that purpose.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 3.4-5 (independent legal advice — genuine recommendation)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4 | Conflicts of Interest

text:
Victor is the principal of an articling student, Winona. Victor instructs Winona to prepare and file a motion for summary judgment. Winona drafts the motion record, which contains a legal error in the applicable test for summary judgment — she applied the old test rather than the test as reformulated by the Supreme Court of Canada. Victor does not review the motion record before Winona files it under his name. The motion is dismissed on the merits. Victor tells the Law Society that Winona was enrolled in the bar admission process and had access to research databases, so he assumed she was competent.

Which of the following best describes Victor's professional responsibility in this situation?
opts: Victor is not responsible because Winona, as an articling student in the licensing process, is expected to perform legal work without supervision.
Victor is not responsible because he assumed Winona was competent by virtue of her enrollment in the bar admission program.

Victor breached his supervision obligation under Rule 5.01; a principal cannot file legal documents prepared by an articling student without reviewing them, and assumptions of competence based on enrollment status do not discharge the supervision obligation.
Victor is responsible only if Winona committed the error intentionally.
expl: Rule 5.01 imposes on principal lawyers an obligation to supervise articling students, including reviewing legal work before it is filed on the lawyer's behalf. An articling student's enrollment in the licensing process does not establish competence for any specific legal task — indeed, it is precisely because students are in training that supervision is required. Victor's assumption of competence, without reviewing the motion record, is a breach of the supervision obligation. The error itself — applying an outdated legal test — is the kind of substantive mistake that a reviewing principal would and should catch.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 5.01 (supervision of articling students)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 | Competence and Quality of Service

text:
Lawyer Trevor operates a boutique immigration law practice. A prospective client, Uma, contacts Trevor for assistance with a refugee claim. During the initial intake call, Trevor learns that Uma is a member of a particular religious minority. Trevor declines to take the file, telling Uma he 'prefers to work with clients from his own cultural background.' No other reason for the refusal is given.

Has Trevor breached Rule 6.3-1 of the Rules of Professional Conduct?
opts: No — a lawyer has an absolute right to decline any client for any reason, including personal preference.
No — Rule 6.3-1 applies only to discrimination in employment within the firm, not to client selection decisions.

Yes — Rule 6.3-1 prohibits a lawyer from discriminating against a client or prospective client on grounds including religion; declining to provide legal services to Uma because of her religious background is a breach of the rule.
Yes — but only if Uma can prove that Trevor accepted clients of other religious backgrounds in the past.
expl: Rule 6.3-1 of the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct prohibits a lawyer from discriminating in the provision of legal services on grounds including religion, race, national or ethnic origin, colour, sex, sexual orientation, gender identity or expression, age, disability, and other prohibited grounds under applicable human rights legislation. Declining to take Uma's file on the explicitly stated basis of her religious background is direct discrimination in the provision of legal services, contrary to Rule 6.3-1. While lawyers generally have some discretion in accepting clients, they may not exercise that discretion on discriminatory grounds.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 6.3-1 (discrimination in provision of legal services)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 13 | Marketing, Advertising, and Discrimination

text:
Lawyer Sandra acts for the vendor in a residential real estate closing. She gives a standard solicitor's undertaking to the purchaser's lawyer to discharge the existing mortgage on the property from the closing proceeds. Closing occurs and Sandra receives the full purchase price including sufficient funds to discharge the mortgage. However, she deposits the closing funds into the wrong trust account and does not process the mortgage discharge for three weeks, during which time the lender assesses an additional interest charge that reduces the available funds below the discharge amount. Sandra argues that the shortfall is the lender's fault for the interest calculation.

Is Sandra personally responsible for ensuring the mortgage discharge undertaking is honored, and does the lender's interest calculation excuse the shortfall?
opts: No — Sandra has fulfilled her undertaking by receiving sufficient funds at closing; the subsequent interest charge is the lender's responsibility and releases Sandra from the undertaking.

Yes — Sandra's solicitor's undertaking to discharge the mortgage is her personal professional obligation; she must honor it regardless of the interest shortfall caused by her delay; she must make up any shortfall from her own resources if necessary, and the delay was caused by her error in depositing the funds incorrectly.

No — the undertaking becomes unenforceable when the lender's actions (charging interest) make it impossible to perform with the available funds; Sandra may discharge her obligation by returning the remaining funds to the purchaser.
Yes — Sandra is responsible, but only for the original amount of the mortgage balance at closing; interest charges accrued after closing are outside the scope of the undertaking.
expl: Sandra's solicitor's undertaking to discharge the mortgage is her personal professional commitment. She received sufficient funds at closing to perform the undertaking. The subsequent interest shortfall arose directly from her own error in depositing funds into the wrong trust account and delaying the discharge process for three weeks. A lawyer who causes the conditions that make an undertaking impossible to perform from available funds cannot escape professional responsibility by attributing the consequences to the other party's actions. Sandra must honor the undertaking, including making up any shortfall resulting from her own delay, and she must process the discharge promptly.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 7.2-11 (real estate — mortgage discharge undertaking — personal obligation)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 10 | Undertakings

text:
Hina is a civil litigator. She receives an email that was clearly intended for opposing counsel, containing confidential solicitor-client communications from the opposing party. Hina reads the email before realizing the error. She is uncertain about her obligations.

What are Hina's professional obligations upon inadvertently receiving confidential communications intended for opposing counsel?
opts: Hina may use the information because she received it without any wrongdoing on her part — inadvertent receipt does not create an obligation.
Hina must immediately delete the email and say nothing to protect her client's tactical advantage.

Hina must immediately notify the sender of the inadvertent disclosure, must not read further than necessary to determine that the communication was not intended for her, must not use the confidential information, and must follow the instructions of the sender or the Law Society regarding the communication.
Hina must report the sender's law firm to the Law Society of Ontario for the inadvertent disclosure.
expl: When a lawyer inadvertently receives confidential communications intended for another lawyer, professional obligations require immediate notification of the sender, cessation of further review, non-use of the information, and compliance with the sender's instructions regarding the document. Using the information would violate the opposing party's confidentiality and privilege. Reporting to the LSO is not the required response — notification to the sender is.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 3.3-1
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 3 | Confidentiality

text:
Lawyer Aaron launches an aggressive advertising campaign for his personal injury practice. His television commercials feature actors dressed as opposing insurance company representatives being thrown around and humiliated. The tagline is: 'We'll DESTROY the other side — call Aaron, the Pit Bull of Personal Injury Law.' He also creates a social media presence featuring memes depicting opposing counsel as 'greedy cowards.' Aaron argues that his campaign is effective marketing and that aggressive branding attracts clients in a competitive market.

Does Aaron's marketing campaign raise professional responsibility concerns under the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct?
opts: No — lawyers are entitled to use aggressive marketing language; the profession does not regulate tone or style of advertising as long as factual claims are accurate.
No — Aaron's campaign is aimed at insurance companies and opposing counsel, not at the LSO or the legal system, so it does not implicate professional responsibility.

Yes — Rule 6.1-1 requires a lawyer to uphold the reputation of the legal profession; marketing that demeans opposing parties and opposing counsel, uses derogatory imagery, or employs language unworthy of the profession can constitute conduct unbecoming a lawyer and may violate Rule 4.2-1's requirement that advertising not demean the profession.
Yes — but only if Aaron's opposing counsel actually complains to the LSO about being depicted in the memes.
expl: Rule 6.1-1 requires a lawyer to uphold the reputation of the legal profession and to refrain from conduct that is unbecoming or reflects adversely on the profession. While competitive advertising is permitted, marketing that denigrates opposing parties, depicts opposing counsel in demeaning terms, or uses language and imagery that a reasonable person would find offensive and unworthy of a legal professional raises concerns under both Rule 6.1-1 and the Commentary to Rule 4.2-1. The tagline 'Pit Bull of Personal Injury Law' and memes depicting opposing counsel as 'greedy cowards' cross the line from assertive marketing into conduct that demeans the profession and opposing parties. The effectiveness of the campaign in attracting clients is not a defence to a professional conduct concern.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 6.1-1 (reputation of the profession); Rule 4.2-1 Commentary (advertising — dignity of profession)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 13 | Marketing, Advertising, and Discrimination

text:
Wendy is asked to act for two siblings purchasing a property as joint tenants. The transaction is simple and the siblings are cooperative. Wendy's assistant prepares a single retainer letter addressed to 'The Clients' describing the scope of services and fees. Both siblings sign the letter. No other specific joint retainer disclosures are made. The transaction closes without incident.

Has Wendy fulfilled her Rule 3.4-5 obligations by having both clients sign a single retainer letter addressed to 'The Clients'?
opts: Yes — a single retainer letter signed by both clients satisfies Rule 3.4-5 as long as both signatures are obtained.

No — Rule 3.4-5 requires that each client be separately and individually advised of the joint retainer nature, the loss of confidentiality between them, the risk of withdrawal, and the recommendation of independent legal advice; a generic letter to 'The Clients' may not constitute adequate individual disclosure to each client.
Yes — because the transaction proceeded smoothly without any conflict, the absence of specific joint retainer disclosure is immaterial.
No — Rule 3.4-5 prohibits a lawyer from acting for two siblings in the same property purchase transaction under any circumstances.
expl: Rule 3.4-5 requires that each client be individually advised of all four elements of the joint retainer disclosure. A generic letter addressed to 'The Clients' that does not specifically set out the required disclosures is likely insufficient even if both clients sign it. The smooth completion of the transaction does not retroactively satisfy the disclosure requirement — the obligation is procedural and must be fulfilled at the outset.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 3.4-5
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4 | Conflicts of Interest

text:
Sole practitioner Marcus relies on his memory and a paper calendar to track limitation periods and court deadlines across his busy 120-file practice areas spanning litigation, real estate, and wills. He has never used a tickler system. A client's two-year limitation period for a slip and fall claim expires without Marcus filing the statement of claim, because he forgot to record the date when intake occurred. Marcus argues that no rule specifically requires him to use a tickler system.

Is Marcus's argument that no specific rule requires use of a tickler system a complete answer to his professional responsibility exposure?
opts: Yes — no LSO rule specifically mandates use of any particular file management system; Marcus's reliance on memory and a paper calendar is a matter of personal practice preference.
Yes — Marcus is only exposed to civil liability to the client for the missed deadline; no professional responsibility consequences arise from the choice of file management system.

No — while no rule mandates a specific system, the competence obligation under Rule 3.1-2 requires a lawyer to have adequate systems in place to manage files, track limitation periods, and meet deadlines; relying on memory alone across a 120-file practice is a breach of the competence standard because it is not an adequate system for a practice of that size.
No — but only if this is a second or subsequent limitation period missed; a first-time missed deadline does not constitute a breach of the competence obligation.
expl: Rule 3.1-2 of the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct imposes a competence obligation that includes managing one's practice with appropriate systems. The Commentary to Rule 3.1-2 and LSO practice management guidance recognizes that maintaining a reliable docketing and reminder system for limitation periods and deadlines is part of the infrastructure of competent practice. Relying entirely on memory and a paper calendar across 120 active files is not adequate — the risk of error is foreseeable. The absence of a rule mandating a specific system does not relieve the lawyer of the obligation to have a system that is adequate for the scale of the practice.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 3.1-2 (competence — practice management systems)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 1 | Licensing and Regulation of the Legal Profession

text:
Lawyer Sophia is acting for a plaintiff in a wrongful dismissal action. She holds $5,000 in trust for the client. An expert report is required and the expert invoices $3,000, payable directly to the expert. Sophia wants to pay the expert directly from the trust account on the client's behalf.

Under By-Law 9, may Sophia pay the expert directly from the trust account for this disbursement?
opts: No — all trust funds must be paid out exclusively to the client; Sophia must first transfer funds to her general account and then pay the expert from there.

Yes — a lawyer may withdraw funds from trust to pay legitimate third-party disbursements incurred on behalf of the client, provided the client has authorized the payment and the disbursement is documented; paying the expert directly from trust on the client's behalf is a permitted withdrawal.
No — expert fees are disbursements that the client must pay personally; they cannot be paid from client trust funds.
Yes — Sophia may pay any amount from trust to any third party at any time because trust funds belong to the client.
expl: By-Law 9 permits a lawyer to withdraw funds from trust to pay legitimate third-party disbursements incurred on behalf of the client, such as court filing fees, expert reports, or other authorized expenses. This is a recognized and common purpose for trust withdrawals. The key requirements are that the client has authorized the disbursement, the payment is properly documented in the trust ledger, and the withdrawal does not create or worsen a trust shortage. Paying the expert directly from trust is appropriate and does not require routing the funds through the general account.
caseRef: LSO By-Law 9 (trust withdrawal — permitted for authorized third-party disbursements)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6 | Trust Accounting and Financial Management

text:
Lawyer Zara previously represented a small construction company, BuildRight Inc., in a municipal permit dispute three years ago. The matter settled. BuildRight disclosed to Zara, in the course of that retainer, detailed financial information about its cash flow difficulties and subcontractor payment practices. Zara now receives a request from a subcontractor, StructurePro, to sue BuildRight Inc. for non-payment on a different construction project. Zara concludes that the new matter is factually different from the permit dispute, so no former-client conflict arises.

Is Zara's conclusion that no former-client conflict exists because the two matters are factually different correct?
opts: Yes — Rule 3.4-10 only prohibits acting against a former client on the exact same matter, and StructurePro's claim involves a different construction project.
Yes — because three years have passed and BuildRight's financial information may no longer be current, the risk of prejudice is too speculative to create a conflict.

No — Rule 3.4-10 also extends to related matters; a new matter is 'related' if confidential information received in the prior retainer could be used to the former client's disadvantage in the new matter; BuildRight's disclosed financial and payment practices are potentially relevant to a subcontractor non-payment claim, making the matters related.
No — but Zara may proceed if she implements an effective information barrier to prevent herself from relying on BuildRight's confidential financial information.
expl: Rule 3.4-10 extends beyond the exact same matter to 'related' matters. Two matters are related if confidential information received in the prior retainer is relevant to, or could prejudice the former client in, the new matter. Zara received detailed information about BuildRight's cash flow difficulties and subcontractor payment practices — precisely the type of information that could be exploited in a subcontractor non-payment claim. The matters are related even though the projects differ. An information barrier is not a cure where the same lawyer holds the confidential information.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 3.4-10
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4 | Conflicts of Interest

text:
Lawyer Greta has been in practice for twelve years. She takes a year-long leave of absence and fails to file her annual report or pay her annual LSO membership fee by the deadline. She also fails to complete the minimum number of continuing professional development (CPD) hours required by the Law Society. Greta intends to return to practice after her leave. She believes these obligations are suspended during a leave of absence.

Is Greta correct that her annual fee, annual report, and CPD obligations are automatically suspended during a leave of absence?
opts: Yes — LSO obligations are suspended automatically whenever a lawyer takes a formal leave of absence from practice.
Yes — CPD requirements are suspended for lawyers who take a leave of absence for health or personal reasons, and fees are reduced proportionally.

No — unless Greta takes the formal step of applying for and obtaining non-practising status with the LSO, her obligations to pay annual fees, file an annual report, and meet CPD requirements continue; failure to comply may result in administrative suspension of her licence.
No — but Greta's CPD requirements are automatically waived for the year in which she takes a leave; fee and annual report obligations still apply.
expl: Annual membership fees, the annual report obligation, and CPD requirements are not automatically suspended by a lawyer taking a personal leave of absence from practice. A lawyer who does not intend to practise may apply for non-practising status, which reduces certain obligations. Without formally obtaining non-practising status, Greta remains subject to all obligations as a practising licensee. Failure to pay fees or comply with CPD can lead to administrative suspension of the licence under the Law Society Act.
caseRef: Law Society Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. L.8; LSO Continuing Professional Development requirements; LSO By-Law 4
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 1 | Licensing and Regulation of the Legal Profession

text:
Lawyer Petra stores all client files on a free consumer cloud storage service she uses for personal photos and documents. She chose it for convenience and has not reviewed its terms of service. The service's terms allow the provider to scan stored content for advertising purposes and do not include data-residency commitments. Client files include confidential communications and sensitive personal information.

What obligation does Petra have under Rule 3.3 and its Commentary regarding the use of cloud storage for client files?
opts: Petra has no special obligation — cloud storage is a standard business tool and its use is implicitly authorized once a retainer is established.

Petra must take reasonable steps to ensure that any cloud storage service used for client files maintains client confidentiality; this requires reviewing provider agreements, ensuring adequate data security and encryption, and confirming appropriate data-residency terms; using a consumer service that allows content scanning is likely insufficient.
Petra may use any cloud storage service she wishes as long as she password-protects individual files.
Petra's obligation is satisfied once she notifies clients that their files are stored in the cloud and obtains their general consent to electronic file management.
expl: The Commentary to Rule 3.3 requires lawyers who use technology in their practice to take reasonable steps to ensure client confidentiality is maintained. For cloud storage, this means reviewing the provider's terms of service to confirm the provider does not claim scanning or access rights inconsistent with confidentiality, ensuring files are encrypted both in transit and at rest, and considering data-residency obligations. A free consumer service that scans content for advertising purposes does not provide adequate confidentiality protection for client files. Reasonable steps are required — not merely password protection or generic client consent.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 3.3-1; Commentary on technology and confidentiality
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 3 | Confidentiality

text:
Lawyer Cassie is a corporate solicitor. Her client, a small manufacturing company, is selling a piece of industrial equipment. Cassie is personally interested in purchasing the equipment for her own business venture. She discusses this informally with the client, who says 'sure, that's fine.' No written disclosure is made, the purchase price is not reviewed against fair market value, and the client does not receive any advice about whether the sale to Cassie is appropriate. Cassie purchases the equipment at a price she considers fair.

What obligations did Cassie have under the Rules of Professional Conduct before entering into this business transaction with her client?
opts: Cassie had no special obligation — business transactions between lawyers and clients are like any other commercial deal.

Cassie was required to disclose her personal interest in the transaction in writing, ensure the transaction terms were fair and reasonable to the client, strongly advise the client in writing to seek independent legal advice, and give the client a reasonable opportunity to act on that advice before proceeding.
Cassie's only obligation was to disclose her personal interest orally to the client, which she satisfied by the informal discussion.
Cassie's purchase is permissible because she is not acting as the client's lawyer in the transaction — she is acting as a buyer, which removes the professional obligation.
expl: The Commentary to Rule 3.4 and the LSO's guidance on conflicts in business transactions with clients establishes that a lawyer who enters into a business transaction with a client has heightened obligations. These include: making written disclosure of the personal interest, ensuring the transaction is fair and reasonable to the client, and strongly advising the client in writing to seek independent legal advice, giving the client reasonable time to obtain it. An informal oral 'that's fine' does not satisfy these requirements. The power imbalance between lawyer and client, and the risk of exploitation, require formal protections.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 3.4-24 to 3.4-28 (business transactions with clients)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4 | Conflicts of Interest

text:
Lawyer Gwen is retained by Redwood Holdings Inc. to assist with a commercial real estate acquisition. The sole representative who contacts Gwen is Marcus, who says he is the CEO of Redwood Holdings Inc. Gwen speaks with Marcus, records his name, and confirms his title as CEO via a company email. Gwen does not obtain any certificate of incorporation, corporate registry search, or any information about who beneficially owns or controls Redwood Holdings Inc.

Has Gwen satisfied the client identification and verification requirements for corporate clients under By-Law 7.1, s.23?
opts: Yes — confirming the identity and title of the corporate representative is sufficient verification for a corporate client.

No — By-Law 7.1, s.23 requires a lawyer to confirm the legal existence of the corporation (typically through a corporate registry search or certificate of incorporation) and to identify the corporation's directors and beneficial owners; Gwen has done neither.
Yes — verification of corporate identity is only required for private companies, not for companies that have an identifiable corporate representative.
No — but Gwen's obligation was satisfied when she recorded the CEO's name and corporate email, because a corporate email constitutes a form of official documentation.
expl: By-Law 7.1, s.23 establishes a multi-step process for verifying corporate client identity. A lawyer must: (1) confirm the legal existence of the corporation, typically through a current corporate registry search or certificate of incorporation; (2) obtain the names of the corporation's directors; and (3) identify the beneficial owners — i.e., individuals who own or control 25% or more of the corporation. Gwen has done none of this. Confirming a representative's title via corporate email does not establish that the corporation legally exists, who controls it, or whether there are any beneficial ownership concerns.
caseRef: LSO By-Law 7.1, s.23 (verification — corporations — existence and beneficial owners)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 12 | Client Identification and Verification

text:
Uma is a family lawyer. Two unmarried partners who are about to move in together ask Uma to draft a cohabitation agreement for both of them. Uma is aware that their interests in the agreement will need to be balanced. She is considering how to proceed.

What must Uma do before accepting the joint retainer under Rule 3.4-5?
opts: Simply proceed if both parties orally agree at the first meeting that they are comfortable with joint representation.
