Reinstatement is not automatic — it requires an application even for administrative suspensions. More seriously, Ulrika practised law for six months while suspended. Practising law while suspended is a separate and serious violation constituting unauthorized practice under the Law Society Act and professional misconduct. Paying the fees and completing CPD resolves the original non-compliance but does not immunize Ulrika from discipline for the unauthorized practice during the suspension period.
caseRef: Law Society Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. L.8, s. 49 (reinstatement after suspension); s. 26.1 (unauthorized practice)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 1 | Licensing and Regulation of the Legal Profession

text:
Consider three different scenarios involving lawyers:
(1) Lawyer A provides a thorough legal opinion but makes a genuine error in statutory interpretation that a competent lawyer would not have made, causing the client financial loss.
(2) Lawyer B knowingly assists her client in creating backdated documents to use in ongoing litigation.
(3) Lawyer C bills the client for 40 hours of work that actually took 15 hours, knowing the extra 25 hours are fabricated.
The LSO is assessing whether each scenario constitutes professional misconduct.

Which of the following most accurately describes how the LSO would classify these three scenarios?
opts: All three constitute professional misconduct because any conduct that harms a client is professional misconduct.
Scenario (1) is professional misconduct; Scenarios (2) and (3) are conduct unbecoming because they involve dishonesty outside the practice of law.

Scenario (1) may constitute negligence or incompetence but is not automatically professional misconduct; Scenarios (2) and (3) are professional misconduct because they involve knowing participation in fraud and dishonest billing — intentional violations of core professional obligations.
Only Scenario (3) is professional misconduct; Scenario (2) involves client conduct that the lawyer facilitated, which is the client's responsibility, and Scenario (1) is merely negligence.
expl: The Law Society Act and the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct draw a meaningful distinction between negligence and professional misconduct. A lawyer who makes a genuine interpretive error, even a significant one, may be negligent — but negligence alone does not automatically constitute professional misconduct unless it is of a sufficiently serious character. By contrast, knowingly assisting a client in creating backdated documents (fraud) and deliberately billing for time never worked (dishonest billing / theft from client) are intentional violations of core professional obligations that fall squarely within professional misconduct. Intentional dishonesty and fraud in the course of practice are hallmarks of professional misconduct.
caseRef: Law Society Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. L.8, s. 33; Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 5.1 (professional misconduct — examples: fraud assistance, dishonest billing)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 11 | Complaints and Discipline

text:
Javeria was the long-time solicitor for a client who has recently died. The deceased's estate executor now approaches Javeria and formally requests all communications between Javeria and the deceased, stating that the communications are needed to assist with estate administration and to resolve a dispute about the deceased's intentions.

Does the duty of confidentiality survive the client's death, and who — if anyone — may authorize disclosure to the executor?
opts: No — the duty of confidentiality ends upon the client's death because the purpose of the duty is to protect a living client.
Yes — confidentiality survives death absolutely; no one can authorize disclosure of the deceased client's communications.

Yes — confidentiality and privilege survive death; however, the executor, as the legal representative of the deceased client's estate, is entitled to waive confidentiality and privilege for purposes of estate administration; Javeria may disclose to the executor who has proper legal authority to waive.
Yes — confidentiality survives death, and only a court order can compel disclosure; the executor has no authority to waive privilege or confidentiality.
expl: Confidentiality and solicitor-client privilege generally survive the death of the client. However, the executor of the estate steps into the shoes of the deceased for purposes of the estate and can waive privilege and confidentiality on behalf of the estate. This allows the executor to access communications relevant to the administration of the estate without requiring a court order.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 3.3-1
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 3 | Confidentiality

text:
Lawyer Frances represents an employee in a wrongful dismissal action against her former employer. The employer is represented by in-house legal counsel. Frances sends a letter directly to the employer's CEO — bypassing in-house counsel — to propose a settlement meeting, believing that the CEO has the ultimate authority to settle and that in-house counsel is obstructing resolution. In-house counsel objects to the direct contact.

Was Frances's direct letter to the CEO permissible under Rule 7.2-1 given that the employer is represented by in-house counsel?
opts: Yes — in-house counsel represents the corporation as an institution, not the individual employees who make up the corporation; the CEO is not a 'represented party' for the purposes of Rule 7.2-1.
Yes — where the lawyer believes that opposing counsel is obstructing settlement, direct contact with the represented party to propose settlement is a recognized exception to Rule 7.2-1.

No — Rule 7.2-1 prohibits direct contact with a person who is represented by counsel in the matter without the consent of that counsel; an employer represented by in-house counsel is a 'person represented by a lawyer' — the CEO, as a senior officer of the represented organization, falls within the scope of the rule; Frances's direct letter to the CEO violated Rule 7.2-1.
Yes — the rule against contacting represented parties only applies to individual persons, not corporations and their officers.
expl: Rule 7.2-1 of the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct prohibits a lawyer from communicating directly with a person known to be represented by another lawyer in the matter, without the other lawyer's knowledge and consent. When the opposing party is a corporation represented by in-house counsel, senior officers of the corporation — including the CEO — are covered by the rule because they speak for the corporation in the matter. Frances's belief that in-house counsel is obstructing settlement does not create an exception; she should have sought to resolve the issue through proper channels, such as a case conference or by raising the matter with the court.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 7.2-1 (no direct contact with represented party — including corporate officers)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8 | Dealing with Opposing Parties and Unrepresented Persons

text:
Indira is a personal injury lawyer. She enters into a contingency fee agreement with a client injured in a car accident. The agreement, signed by both parties, provides that Indira will receive 33% of any recovery. The agreement is in writing and sets out the method for calculating the fee but does not describe the potential legal costs that may be recovered from the defendant and how those recovered costs will be handled. The matter settles for $300,000.

What formal requirements for contingency fee agreements are imposed under the Rules of Professional Conduct and related legislation, and has Indira satisfied them?
opts: A contingency fee agreement need only be signed by the client; no description of how recovered costs will be treated is required.

Contingency fee agreements must be in writing, signed by both parties, and must also address how legal costs recovered from the opposing party will be applied; Indira's agreement may be deficient for omitting this information.
The only requirement for a valid contingency fee agreement is that it must not exceed 40% of the recovery.
Contingency fee agreements require pre-approval by the Law Society of Ontario before they are binding on the client.
expl: Rule 3.6-2 and the Solicitors Act (R.S.O. 1990, c. S.15) require that contingency fee agreements be in writing and signed by the client and the lawyer. The agreement must also disclose how any legal costs recovered from the opposing party will be treated — whether they offset the contingency percentage or are paid in addition to it. This disclosure protects clients from unexpected fee arrangements at settlement. Indira's agreement is deficient because it does not address how recovered costs will be handled.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 3.6-2; Solicitors Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. S.15
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 5 | Fees, Billing, and Financial Dealings with Clients

text:
Quincy is acting for a buyer in a residential real estate transaction. Before the deal closes, the seller's lawyer withdraws and the seller asks Quincy to also represent the seller in completing the same transaction. Both parties want the deal to close quickly.

What is the nature of the conflict and how should Quincy respond to the seller's request?
opts: Quincy may act for both buyer and seller if both parties provide informed consent, since they have the same goal of completing the transaction.

Quincy must decline to act for the seller; in the same real estate transaction, buyer and seller have directly adverse interests regarding price, conditions, deposit, representations, and risk allocation; this is a conflict that cannot be resolved by consent.
Quincy may act for both if he uses separate files and ensures he does not share confidential information between the two clients.
There is no conflict because both parties want the transaction to close; their shared objective means their interests are aligned.
expl: In the same real estate transaction, the buyer and seller have directly adverse interests on key terms including price, deposit conditions, representations and warranties, and risk allocation. This is a paradigmatic example of a non-consentable conflict. A lawyer cannot act for both buyer and seller in the same transaction regardless of their consent, because the lawyer cannot provide undivided loyalty to both simultaneously. Information barriers are ineffective when the same lawyer acts for opposing parties in the same matter.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 3.4-1
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4 | Conflicts of Interest

text:
A client arrives at Lawyer Nadia's office with $9,000 in cash as a retainer for a real estate transaction. The client explains that she prefers to pay in cash and does not want to go to the bank. Nadia is about to accept the cash when her paralegal mentions there may be a rule about accepting large amounts of cash.

What does By-Law 9 provide regarding a lawyer's ability to receive cash from a client, and how does it apply to Nadia's situation?
opts: Lawyers may accept any amount of cash from a client as long as the funds are deposited to trust and a receipt is provided.
By-Law 9 prohibits a lawyer from receiving or accepting cash in excess of $7,500 per client matter; since the $9,000 exceeds this limit, Nadia cannot accept the cash payment.
Cash payments are only restricted if the lawyer suspects the client of money laundering; since Nadia has no suspicion, she may accept the $9,000.
By-Law 9 permits cash payments up to $10,000 per transaction; $9,000 is within the permitted limit.
expl: By-Law 9 prohibits a lawyer from receiving or accepting cash — physical currency — in excess of $7,500 in respect of any one client matter. This restriction is part of Ontario's anti-money laundering framework applicable to legal professionals. The prohibition applies per matter, not per transaction. Since the $9,000 cash payment exceeds the $7,500 limit, Nadia must decline to accept the cash and request that the client provide payment by another acceptable means (e.g., certified cheque, wire transfer). Accepting a cash payment above the limit is itself a violation of By-Law 9 regardless of whether there is any suspicion of money laundering.
caseRef: LSO By-Law 9 (no-cash rule — $7,500 limit per client matter)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6 | Trust Accounting and Financial Management

text:
Lawyer Omar has been acting for the defendant in a civil action. Discoveries are complete and a trial date has been set for six months from now. Omar and his client have had a serious falling out — the client has written Omar an abusive letter and announced she is planning to retain new counsel. Omar wishes to formally withdraw from the record.

What procedural step is required for Omar to withdraw from a civil litigation matter that is on the record with a trial date?
opts: Omar may file a Notice of Change of Solicitor immediately and his withdrawal is complete upon filing.
Omar must bring a motion to the court for leave to withdraw and cannot be removed from the record until the court grants leave.
Omar must obtain the client's signed consent and then file the appropriate notice; once the client has announced she is retaining new counsel, no court leave is required.
Omar must remain as counsel of record until the new lawyer files a Notice of Change of Solicitor on behalf of the client; he cannot withdraw unilaterally.
expl: In Ontario civil litigation, the procedure for withdrawal depends on the circumstances. Where a client is retaining new counsel and will not be left unrepresented, and where the client consents, the standard procedure is for the new lawyer to file a Notice of Change of Solicitor — or for the withdrawing lawyer to file a Notice of Intention to Act in Person if the client will be self-represented. Court leave is generally required only when the client is not obtaining new representation and would be left unrepresented at a critical stage, or in criminal proceedings. In Omar's case, since the client has announced she is retaining new counsel, the appropriate path is for the new counsel to file the change notice; no court motion is required.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 3.7-8 (withdrawal from proceedings — civil vs. criminal distinction)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 9 | Withdrawal from Representation

text:
Lawyer Sabrina has a contingency fee agreement with a client in a motor vehicle accident case. The agreement provides that Sabrina will receive 33% of the gross settlement 'including any amounts for costs or disbursements recovered from the defendant.' The case settles for $200,000, of which $20,000 was identified by the defendant's insurer as reimbursement for the client's medical and expert report costs. Sabrina proposes to apply her 33% to the full $200,000, including the $20,000 in disbursement reimbursements.

What is the most significant professional concern with Sabrina's proposed fee calculation?
opts: Sabrina cannot charge any contingency fee on a settlement; she may only charge on trial verdicts.

Charging a contingency percentage on funds specifically identified as reimbursement for disbursements already incurred may result in double recovery — Sabrina would be both recovering the disbursements themselves and charging a percentage fee on top of them; the agreement and applicable rules must be consulted to determine whether this is permissible.

Sabrina's agreement is valid and she may take 33% of the full settlement including the disbursement reimbursement, without any concern, because the agreement says 'including any amounts for costs or disbursements.'
Disbursement reimbursements from the opposing party always go directly to the client and cannot form part of the contingency fee base under any circumstances.
expl: Applying a contingency percentage to funds specifically identified as reimbursement for out-of-pocket disbursements already paid by or on behalf of the client can result in the lawyer effectively being paid twice for the same expense — once through the disbursement recovery and again as a percentage of that recovery. By-Law 9 and fairness principles require clarity in contingency fee agreements about how disbursement recoveries will be treated. Where the agreement's language is ambiguous or where its application would result in a windfall, the lawyer should seek the client's informed consent to the calculation and should be prepared to have the fee assessed.
caseRef: LSO By-Law 9 (contingency fee — disbursements); Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 3.6-1 (fair and reasonable fees)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 5 | Fees, Billing, and Financial Dealings with Clients

text:
Lawyer Priya practises law on a part-time basis — she works approximately two days per week at a small general practice firm. She holds a full barrister and solicitor licence with the LSO. At the end of the year, she has not completed any CPD hours, reasoning that because she only works part-time her CPD obligation must be correspondingly reduced.

Is Priya correct that her CPD obligations are reduced because she is a part-time practitioner?
opts: Yes — the LSO's CPD requirements are prorated based on the number of hours a lawyer works per week.
Yes — part-time lawyers are exempt from CPD requirements provided they declare part-time status on their annual report.

No — the LSO's CPD requirements apply to all practising licensees regardless of whether they practise full-time or part-time; Priya must complete the minimum annual CPD hours, including the required professionalism component, regardless of the number of days per week she practises.
No — but Priya may defer up to half of her CPD requirement to the following year by filing a hardship exemption.
expl: The LSO's CPD requirements apply to all practising licensees holding a barrister and solicitor licence, whether they practise full-time or part-time. There is no proportional reduction or proration of CPD hours based on the hours worked per week. A part-time lawyer must complete the same minimum annual CPD hours as a full-time lawyer, including the mandatory professionalism component. If Priya does not wish to comply with CPD requirements, she must formally apply for non-practising status — she cannot simply reduce her obligations by practising fewer hours.
caseRef: Law Society Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. L.8; LSO By-Law 6.1 (Continuing Professional Development)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 1 | Licensing and Regulation of the Legal Profession

text:
Lawyer Ingrid acts for a purchaser in a commercial real estate closing. She is instructed by email, purportedly from the vendor's lawyer, to wire $2,000,000 from trust to a new bank account number that differs from what was used in prior communications. Ingrid is pressed for time and, without verifying the instruction by phone, processes the wire transfer. The email was sent by a fraudster who had compromised the vendor's lawyer's email account. The funds are lost.

What additional safeguard should Ingrid have taken before processing the electronic trust transfer under By-Law 9 and professional standards?
opts: Ingrid had no further obligation — an email instruction from a lawyer is a valid and sufficient authorization for any trust wire transfer.
Ingrid should have obtained written direction from her own client before proceeding; the vendor's lawyer's email is not a relevant authorization for disbursing funds held in trust for the purchaser.

Before processing an electronic trust transfer — particularly where banking details have changed from prior communications — Ingrid was required to independently verify the instruction through a separate, confirmed communication channel (such as a phone call to a previously known number); failure to verify a changed wire transfer instruction is a recognized fraud vector that creates professional liability.
Ingrid's only obligation was to ensure the transfer instruction was in writing; since the email constituted a writing, she satisfied her requirements.
expl: Electronic trust wire transfers — especially those involving changed banking details — are a known vector for email compromise fraud. By-Law 9 requires that trust transfers be authorized and properly documented, but professional standards and best practices require lawyers to independently verify changes to wire transfer instructions through a confirmed, out-of-band communication channel (e.g., a phone call to a previously known and verified number). Relying solely on an email instruction — without verification — when the banking details have changed is professionally unreasonable and can result in personal liability for the lawyer if client funds are lost through fraud.
caseRef: LSO By-Law 9 (electronic trust transfers — authorization and safeguards); LSO Practice Advisories on wire transfer fraud
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6 | Trust Accounting and Financial Management

text:
During an initial consultation, a prospective client tells Lawyer Margot that he has devised a scheme to fraudulently transfer ownership of a corporation's shares to himself and wants her to prepare the necessary share transfer documents. He explains the mechanism of the fraud in detail to obtain her assistance. Margot declines to assist. The prospective client later claims that all communications during the initial consultation are protected by solicitor-client privilege and cannot be disclosed to the corporation's other shareholders.

Are the communications in this initial consultation protected by solicitor-client privilege?
opts: Yes — all communications during an initial consultation with a prospective client are automatically protected by solicitor-client privilege regardless of content.
Yes — the privilege attaches because Margot was providing legal advice and she ultimately declined the retainer, so no crime was facilitated.

No — the crime-fraud exception to solicitor-client privilege removes protection from communications where the client is seeking to use the lawyer's assistance to commit or plan a future fraud; communications made for the purpose of obtaining legal help to facilitate a fraudulent scheme are not protected.
Yes — the crime-fraud exception only applies once a crime has actually been committed; since no crime occurred, the communications remain privileged.
expl: Solicitor-client privilege does not protect communications made for the purpose of obtaining legal assistance to facilitate a future crime or fraud. This is the crime-fraud exception: when a client seeks a lawyer's assistance to carry out a planned criminal or fraudulent act, the communications in furtherance of that purpose are not privileged. The exception applies at the time the communication is made — it turns on the client's purpose. The fact that Margot declined to help does not restore privilege over communications that were made to obtain assistance with a fraud. The exception is not triggered by past crimes but by the purpose of the communication itself.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 3.3-1; crime-fraud exception to solicitor-client privilege
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 3 | Confidentiality

text:
Lawyer Thomas is representing a defendant in a criminal matter. He discovers during the retainer that his client has been lying to him about a material fact in a way that would cause Thomas to make misrepresentations to the court. Thomas decides he must withdraw. At the next court appearance, the judge asks Thomas why he is withdrawing on short notice without replacement counsel. The Crown counsel presses the matter, suggesting the withdrawal may indicate the client's guilt.

What may Thomas disclose to the court about the reasons for his withdrawal?
opts: Thomas must provide the full reason for his withdrawal to the court so the judge can assess whether the withdrawal is justified.

Thomas may only disclose that he is withdrawing for professional reasons and must not reveal any confidential information about the client in explaining his withdrawal; the duty of confidentiality prevents him from disclosing the substance of the client's conduct that necessitated the withdrawal.
Thomas should disclose to the Crown that the client lied to him, as this protects the administration of justice from being misled.
Thomas may disclose the reason for withdrawal if the court orders him to, but not voluntarily.
expl: Rule 3.3-1 of the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct imposes a duty of confidentiality that continues even when a lawyer withdraws. A lawyer who withdraws for professional reasons — including because the client's conduct would require the lawyer to mislead the court — must decline to provide reasons that would disclose confidential information. Thomas may tell the court only that he is withdrawing for professional reasons, without disclosing the substance of the client's conduct. The duty of confidentiality is not overridden by judicial curiosity or Crown pressure.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rules 3.3-1, 3.7-9 (confidentiality on withdrawal)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 3 | Confidentiality

text:
Lawyer Grant is preparing for a civil trial. His client will testify that a written invoice in the file is genuine and was signed by the other party. Grant has a nagging suspicion that the invoice may have been altered, but he has no proof — there are no forensic tests, no admissions, and no contradictory evidence. The client insists the invoice is authentic. Grant is trying to decide whether he can call his client to testify about the invoice.

Under Rule 5.1-2, may Grant call his client to testify about the invoice in these circumstances?
opts: No — even a suspicion that evidence may be false is enough to prevent a lawyer from adducing it; Rule 5.1-2 prohibits adducing any evidence the lawyer is not certain is truthful.

Yes — Rule 5.1-2 prohibits adducing evidence the lawyer knows to be false, not evidence the lawyer merely suspects may be false; Grant does not have knowledge of falsity and may call the evidence, but should remain alert.
No — Grant must conduct forensic testing before calling the client to testify; failing to verify the authenticity of documentary evidence violates Rule 5.1-2.
Yes — Grant may call the evidence because his duty of loyalty to his client requires him to advance the client's position on all available evidence regardless of any personal suspicion.
expl: Rule 5.1-2 of the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct prohibits a lawyer from adducing evidence the lawyer knows to be false. The critical word is 'knows' — the prohibition does not extend to evidence the lawyer merely suspects or has doubts about. If Grant has a suspicion but no actual knowledge that the invoice is false, he is not prohibited from calling his client to testify about it. The lawyer must act on actual knowledge, not unsubstantiated suspicion. Of course, if Grant later acquires actual knowledge that the invoice is a forgery, the analysis changes entirely.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 5.1-2 (presentation of evidence — knowing vs. suspecting evidence is false)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7 | Duties to Tribunals and Third Parties

text:
Lawyer Archie has been acting for a client in a complex breach of contract action for 18 months. The trial is scheduled to begin in three weeks. The client has consistently ignored Archie's advice on litigation strategy and has given instructions that Archie believes are not in the client's best interests. The client has also failed to pay Archie's last three invoices totalling $15,000. Archie wants to withdraw from the retainer immediately.

Which of the following most accurately describes Archie's rights and obligations regarding withdrawal under Rule 3.7-1?
opts: Archie must continue representing the client until the trial concludes because withdrawal this close to trial would prejudice the client, regardless of non-payment or strategic disagreements.
Archie may withdraw immediately without any obligation to the client because non-payment of fees is an absolute right to withdraw.

Under Rule 3.7-1, non-payment of fees and loss of confidence in the lawyer-client relationship are grounds for optional withdrawal, but Archie must give reasonable notice and withdraw at an appropriate time in the proceedings to minimize prejudice to the client; a three-week window before trial requires Archie to consider whether immediate withdrawal is appropriate or whether adequate time remains to find replacement counsel.
Archie cannot withdraw at all because a lawyer must always see a matter to completion once retained.
expl: Rule 3.7-1 provides grounds for optional withdrawal, including loss of confidence in the lawyer-client relationship and client failure to pay fees. However, the right to withdraw is constrained by the obligation to give reasonable notice and to withdraw at a time and in a manner that minimizes prejudice to the client. With trial three weeks away, Archie must carefully assess whether withdrawal at this stage would prejudice the client's ability to obtain replacement counsel and prepare for trial. The closer to trial, the more compelling the reason for withdrawal must be.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 3.7-1
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 9 | Withdrawal from Representation

text:
Lawyer Neil acts for a supplier in a payment dispute with a restaurant owner. Neil sends a demand letter stating: 'Unless your client pays the outstanding $18,000 within 15 days, my client will file a complaint with the Ontario Ministry of Labour reporting the underpayment of wages to your client's employees that we discovered during our business relationship, and will also report the suspected tax irregularities to the Canada Revenue Agency.' Neil's client has genuine information about both issues.

Does Neil's demand letter violate Rule 7.2-7 even though the threatened complaints involve regulatory matters, not criminal prosecution?
opts: No — Rule 7.2-7 applies only to threats of criminal proceedings; threatening regulatory or administrative complaints is permitted as a legitimate negotiation tactic.
No — because the underlying information about the labour and tax issues is genuine, Neil is entitled to inform the opposing party of his client's reporting options.

Yes — Rule 7.2-7 prohibits threatening to initiate criminal or regulatory proceedings against a person primarily as a means to obtain a civil advantage; conditioning the withholding of regulatory complaints on civil payment is equally prohibited whether the threatened proceedings are criminal or regulatory in nature.
Yes — but only if the restaurant owner has a lawyer; threats of regulatory proceedings against unrepresented parties are assessed under a different standard.
expl: Rule 7.2-7 of the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct expressly extends beyond criminal proceedings to include the threat to initiate regulatory proceedings as a lever to obtain civil advantage. The rule prohibits threatening 'to initiate or proceed with a criminal charge, or to make a complaint to a regulatory authority' primarily to gain a civil concession. The fact that Neil's client has genuine information about the violations does not authorize using those facts as bargaining chips conditioned on civil payment. The prohibited conduct is the conditioning of non-reporting on payment — not the reporting itself.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 7.2-7 (threatening criminal or regulatory proceedings to gain civil advantage)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8 | Dealing with Opposing Parties and Unrepresented Persons

text:
Lawyer Renata operates a busy practice and maintains a mixed trust account for 40 active clients. She tracks all trust money received in a single general ledger entry for the mixed trust account, showing total deposits and withdrawals chronologically, but does not maintain a separate ledger entry for each individual client. When asked by the LSO during a compliance audit what balance is held in trust for a specific client, Renata cannot produce that information without extensive manual calculation.

Has Renata complied with the trust ledger requirements of By-Law 9?
opts: Yes — a single combined trust account ledger is sufficient under By-Law 9 as long as the total trust balance is accurately tracked.

No — By-Law 9 requires that a lawyer maintain a separate trust ledger for each client whose funds are held in the mixed trust account; the ledger must show all trust transactions for that specific client and must allow the lawyer to identify the balance held for each client at any time.
Yes — the requirement for a per-client trust ledger only applies to lawyers handling real estate or estate matters; litigation trusts may be tracked in aggregate.
No — but the per-client ledger is only required if the trust account holds funds for more than 20 clients simultaneously.
expl: By-Law 9 requires that a lawyer maintain a separate trust ledger account for each client or matter for which trust funds are held. The per-client trust ledger must record all receipts and disbursements of trust funds for that client and must be maintained in a way that allows the lawyer to identify the balance held for each client at any time. A single combined chronological entry for all clients is non-compliant — it makes it impossible to determine individual client balances without extensive manual reconstruction, which is precisely the problem By-Law 9's record-keeping requirements are designed to prevent.
caseRef: LSO By-Law 9, s. 8 (client trust ledger — per-client accounting)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6 | Trust Accounting

text:
Lawyer Elspeth is a sole practitioner. She works with a part-time administrative assistant who has unrestricted access to all client files, emails, and voicemails on the shared office computer. She has never reviewed what information the assistant can access. A client later discovers that the assistant accessed and disclosed highly sensitive personal information from the client's file to a third party without authorization.

Was Elspeth's failure to implement access controls on her office systems a breach of her duty of confidentiality?
opts: No — Elspeth is not responsible for unauthorized disclosures made by her employees; only the assistant is liable.
No — a sole practitioner with a small office is exempt from the technology safeguards required of larger firms.

Yes — Elspeth's duty of confidentiality under Rule 3.3-1 includes an obligation to take reasonable steps to ensure that office systems, including access controls, are organized to protect client information from unauthorized access by staff; failing to implement controls that would have limited the assistant's access to only necessary files is a breach of that duty.
Yes, but only because Elspeth used a shared computer — the violation would not have occurred if she used separate computers for each file.
expl: Rule 3.3-1 requires a lawyer to take reasonable steps to protect client confidentiality. This obligation extends to the organization of the lawyer's practice — including implementing appropriate access controls and supervision of staff. Giving a part-time assistant unrestricted access to all client files without any controls or review is a failure to take reasonable steps. The Commentary to Rule 3.3 makes clear that lawyers must organize their practices to ensure confidentiality is maintained at all levels, including by staff. A sole practitioner is not exempt from this obligation.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 3.3-1; Commentary on office security and staff supervision
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 3 | Confidentiality

text:
Lawyer Anton has been representing his longtime client, a small restaurant owner, for several years. The client is experiencing financial difficulty. The client asks Anton if he can lend the client $20,000 from Anton's personal funds to help the client through a cash flow crisis. Anton is considering whether to agree.

Which of the following best describes Anton's professional responsibility obligations if he lends money to his client?
opts: Anton may freely lend money to clients because this shows commitment to the lawyer-client relationship.
Anton may lend money to the client as long as the loan is documented in writing and bears a reasonable interest rate.

Lending money to a current client creates an own-interest conflict under Rule 3.4-1; Anton should not lend money to his client, and if he proceeds despite the risk, the client must receive independent legal advice about the terms of the loan and its implications for the retainer.
Anton may lend money to the client provided he discloses the loan to the Law Society of Ontario within 30 days.
expl: Lending money to a client creates an own-interest conflict under Rule 3.4-1 because the lawyer's personal financial interests as a creditor may conflict with the client's interests as a debtor and as a client seeking legal advice. The Commentary to the Rules makes clear that a lawyer generally should not lend money to a client. If such a transaction occurs, the client should receive independent legal advice to ensure the transaction is not the product of the lawyer's superior bargaining position.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 3.4-1
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4 | Conflicts of Interest

text:
Lawyer Yul is acting for a commercial landlord. In his dealings with the tenant's lawyer, Yul frequently sends correspondence that is deliberately misleading about the state of negotiations, makes false representations about the landlord's settlement authority, and uses deliberately threatening language to intimidate opposing counsel. Yul justifies this by saying it is aggressive but effective advocacy.

Does Yul's conduct comply with the Rules of Professional Conduct regarding dealings with opposing counsel?
opts: Yes — lawyers are entitled to be aggressive advocates and there is no rule requiring courtesy or good faith toward opposing counsel.

No — Rule 7.1-1 requires a lawyer to be courteous and act in good faith in all dealings with other lawyers and parties; deliberately misleading opposing counsel, making false representations, and using threatening language constitutes sharp practice and professional misconduct.
Yes — aggressive negotiation tactics, including deliberate misrepresentation of settlement authority, are standard commercial practice accepted by the Rules.
No — but Yul's conduct is only problematic if opposing counsel formally objects; absent an objection, aggressive conduct is permissible.
expl: Rule 7.1-1 of the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct requires a lawyer to be courteous, civil, and act in good faith in all professional dealings. The Commentary to Rule 7.1-1 specifically addresses sharp practice — the use of tactics designed to mislead, surprise, or take unfair advantage of opposing counsel. Making false representations about settlement authority and deliberately misleading opposing counsel are prohibited. Yul's conduct constitutes sharp practice and a breach of the Rules regardless of whether it is 'effective.'
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 7.1-1
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8 | Dealing with Opposing Parties and Unrepresented Persons

text:
Sofia retains a lawyer, Takeshi, specifically to review and comment on a commercial contract she has already negotiated. They agree in writing that Takeshi's retainer is limited to document review only — he will not attend negotiations, advise on business strategy, or recommend whether Sofia should sign. Sofia later complains that Takeshi should have flagged that the pricing structure, while not legally defective, was commercially disadvantageous and that she should have renegotiated before signing.

Did Takeshi breach his competence obligation by not advising on the commercial desirability of the pricing structure?
opts: Yes — a lawyer who reviews a contract must advise on all aspects of the document, including its commercial terms, regardless of the agreed scope of the retainer.

No — Rule 3.2-1A permits a lawyer and client to agree in writing to limit the scope of the retainer; provided the limitation was reasonable in the circumstances and clearly communicated, Takeshi's obligation was confined to document review and he had no duty to advise beyond that scope.
No — but only because Sofia is a sophisticated commercial client who should have known to ask about commercial terms.
Yes — a limited-scope retainer is only valid in litigation matters, not in transactional contexts.
expl: Rule 3.2-1A of the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct permits a lawyer and client to agree to limit the scope of the representation, provided the limitation is reasonable in the circumstances, clearly communicated, and confirmed in writing. Where such an agreement is validly in place, the lawyer's competence obligation is assessed against the agreed limited scope. Takeshi's retainer was confined to document review; advising on commercial negotiation strategy was expressly excluded. He did not breach his competence obligation by staying within the agreed boundaries.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 3.2-1A (limited-scope retainer)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 | Competence and Quality of Service

text:
Lawyer Dale is a practicing family lawyer in Toronto. In the course of his family law practice, Dale knowingly assists a client in hiding assets from opposing counsel during financial disclosure. Separately, outside his practice, Dale is convicted of shoplifting merchandise worth $75 from a grocery store — an offence unrelated to his legal work. The LSO is investigating both incidents.

Which of the following correctly distinguishes the two categories of discipline that may apply to Dale's conduct under the Law Society Act?
opts: Both the asset-hiding and shoplifting incidents constitute professional misconduct because professional misconduct applies to any wrongful act by a lawyer regardless of whether it occurs in practice.

Knowingly assisting a client in hiding assets is professional misconduct because it occurs in the course of practice and violates professional obligations; the shoplifting conviction is conduct occurring outside practice but may constitute conduct unbecoming a licensee if it is sufficiently serious to reflect adversely on Dale's fitness or on the reputation of the profession.
The shoplifting conviction cannot be the subject of any LSO discipline because it is a matter for the criminal courts; the LSO only has jurisdiction over conduct that occurs in the practice of law.
Both incidents are conduct unbecoming a licensee because conduct unbecoming is a broader category that encompasses all forms of lawyer misconduct.
expl: The Law Society Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. L.8, s. 33 defines two relevant disciplinary categories. 'Professional misconduct' refers to conduct that occurs in the course of the practice of law and violates the obligations of a lawyer — such as knowingly assisting a client in concealing assets during financial disclosure. 'Conduct unbecoming a licensee' covers conduct that, while not necessarily occurring in practice, is so contrary to the expected standards of good character and integrity that it reflects adversely on the lawyer's fitness or on the profession. A criminal conviction for theft, even though it occurs outside practice, can fall into this category. The two categories are distinct and both may result in discipline.
caseRef: Law Society Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. L.8, s. 33 (professional misconduct vs. conduct unbecoming a licensee)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 11 | Complaints and Discipline

text:
Lawyer Ulrich is defending a commercial landlord in a wrongful eviction action. The tenant has strong evidence of the eviction. Ulrich's client instructs him to raise every possible defence, including defences that Ulrich knows have absolutely no legal or factual foundation, simply to delay the proceedings and impose maximum costs on the tenant. Ulrich proceeds as instructed.

Has Ulrich acted in accordance with his duties under the Rules of Professional Conduct?
opts: Yes — a defence lawyer's duty is to zealously advocate for the client by raising all possible defences; Ulrich is obligated to follow his client's instructions.
Yes — the court can assess costs against the landlord at the end of the proceedings, which is the appropriate mechanism to address unfounded defences.

No — Rule 5.1-2 prohibits a lawyer from asserting a position in proceedings where the lawyer knows the position is not supported by law and has no merit; using unfounded defences to delay proceedings and harass the opposing party is a breach of the advocate's duty.
Yes — as long as the unfounded defences are technically available at law, raising them is a legitimate tactical decision within the advocate's discretion.
expl: Rule 5.1-2 of the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct limits the advocate's role by prohibiting the assertion of positions in proceedings that the lawyer knows to be without merit. A lawyer cannot use litigation as a tool of delay and harassment by pleading defences that have no legal or factual foundation. Zealous advocacy does not extend to advancing knowingly baseless positions. The availability of costs as a remedy at the end of proceedings does not excuse the lawyer from complying with Rule 5.1-2 during the proceedings.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 5.1-2
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7 | Duties to Tribunals and Third Parties

text:
Fatima is a sole practitioner who employs two legal assistants. She is very busy and has instructed her legal assistants to handle initial client intake calls, assess whether matters are urgent, and draft routine correspondence to send to opposing parties. She does not review the assistants' correspondence before it is sent. One of the assistants sends an opposing party a letter containing an incorrect legal position that prejudices Fatima's client. Fatima learns of it when the opposing party responds.

Which of the following best describes Fatima's obligations and potential liability under Rule 5.01 of the Rules of Professional Conduct?
opts: Fatima is not liable because non-lawyers act independently and bear their own responsibility for errors they commit.
Fatima is solely liable for the incorrect letter only if she specifically directed the legal assistant to write it.

Under Rule 5.01(1), a lawyer must ensure that non-lawyers working under their supervision comply with the Rules of Professional Conduct; Fatima has breached this obligation by failing to review outgoing correspondence prepared by her assistants, making her professionally responsible for the error.
Fatima is not liable because she properly delegated the task and the legal assistant was at fault for not seeking clarification.
expl: Rule 5.01(1) of the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct requires a lawyer to ensure that non-lawyers working under the lawyer's supervision comply with the Rules of Professional Conduct. The Commentary to Rule 5.01 makes clear that a lawyer must implement systems to review and supervise non-lawyer work. Fatima's failure to review correspondence before it is sent on her behalf is a breach of her supervision obligation — she cannot delegate professional responsibility to unqualified staff.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 5.01(1)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 | Competence and Quality of Service
